name: shekel-weight-standard
description: Mesopotamians adopted the shekel as a unit of weight for silver, an early standard for measuring value.

name: first-promissory-clay-tablets
description: Sumerian scribes recorded loans, interest, and debts on clay tablets, among the earliest written finance.

name: code-hammurabi-interest-rules
description: Babylon's code set legal limits on loan interest rates and rules for debt, an early financial regulation.

name: egyptian-grain-banking
description: Ancient Egypt ran state granaries where stored grain served as deposits and a medium of exchange.

name: phoenician-trade-network
description: Phoenician merchants built a Mediterranean commercial empire dealing in dyes, metals, and luxury goods.

name: athenian-owl-tetradrachm
description: Athens minted silver tetradrachms stamped with an owl, becoming a trusted currency across the Mediterranean.

name: athenian-laurion-silver-mines
description: Rich silver strikes at Laurion funded Athens' navy and coinage, fueling its rise to a sea power.

name: persian-daric-gold-coin
description: Darius I issued the gold daric, a pure coin that standardized money across the vast Persian Empire.

name: roman-denarius-introduced
description: Rome introduced the silver denarius, which became the backbone of its economy for centuries.

name: roman-tax-census-augustus
description: Emperor Augustus ordered empire-wide censuses to assess property and collect taxes more efficiently.

name: roman-aureus-gold-coin
description: Rome's gold aureus circulated alongside the silver denarius, anchoring high-value transactions across the empire.

name: roman-currency-debasement
description: Emperors steadily cut the silver content of coins, triggering inflation that weakened the Roman economy.

name: byzantine-solidus-introduced
description: Constantine issued the gold solidus, a stable coin that dominated Mediterranean trade for centuries.

name: islamic-gold-dinar-minted
description: Caliph Abd al-Malik issued the gold dinar, establishing a distinct Islamic currency across the caliphate.

name: song-dynasty-jiaozi-paper
description: China's Song dynasty issued jiaozi, the first government-backed paper banknotes in world history.

name: hawala-money-transfer
description: Medieval Islamic merchants developed hawala, an informal network for transferring money without moving cash.

name: domesday-tax-survey
description: William the Conqueror surveyed England's lands and wealth to assess taxes, producing the Domesday Book.

name: english-exchequer-tally
description: England's royal Exchequer used a checkered cloth and tally sticks to audit and collect crown revenues.

name: kublai-khan-paper-currency
description: Kublai Khan enforced paper currency across China, amazing Marco Polo with money made from mulberry bark.

name: english-pound-sterling-origin
description: Henry II issued the Tealby penny, standardizing English silver coinage that defined the pound sterling.

name: venetian-state-bond-prestiti
description: Venice consolidated its debts into tradable government bonds, an early form of the public bond market.

name: first-marine-insurance-policy
description: Genoese merchants signed early marine insurance contracts to protect cargo against losses at sea.

name: double-entry-pacioli-treatise
description: Luca Pacioli published the first printed description of double-entry bookkeeping, the basis of accounting.

name: potosi-silver-floods-spain
description: Vast silver from Potosi in Bolivia poured into Spain, reshaping global trade and sparking price inflation.

name: spanish-price-revolution
description: A flood of New World silver caused sustained inflation across Europe over the sixteenth century.

name: spanish-pieces-of-eight
description: Spain's silver peso, the piece of eight, became the first truly global currency used across continents.

name: greshams-law-coinage
description: Financier Thomas Gresham observed that bad money drives good money out of circulation.

name: royal-exchange-london-opens
description: Thomas Gresham founded the Royal Exchange, giving London merchants a central place to trade and finance.

name: bank-of-amsterdam-founded
description: The Bank of Amsterdam created a stable public deposit bank that helped make the city a financial hub.

name: first-stock-dividend-voc
description: The Dutch East India Company paid shareholders the first regular dividends on publicly traded stock.

name: swedish-banknotes-issued
description: Stockholms Banco issued the first paper banknotes in Europe, an experiment that soon overstretched.

name: first-cheques-england
description: London goldsmith-bankers issued early written cheques, letting depositors pay others from their accounts.

name: lloyds-coffeehouse-insurance
description: Edward Lloyd's London coffeehouse became a hub for marine insurers, growing into Lloyd's of London.

name: gold-standard-newton
description: Isaac Newton, master of the Mint, set a gold price that effectively put Britain on the gold standard.

name: first-paper-money-colonial
description: Massachusetts Bay issued the first government paper money in the American colonies to pay soldiers.

name: first-building-society
description: Ketley's of Birmingham formed the first building society, pooling members' savings to finance their homes.

name: continental-dollar-collapse
description: Over-issued paper Continentals lost nearly all value during the American Revolution, fueling distrust of paper money.

name: us-dollar-established
description: The Coinage Act created the US dollar and a national mint, setting the new nation's monetary system.

name: new-york-stock-exchange-founded
description: Brokers signed the Buttonwood Agreement under a tree on Wall Street, founding the New York Stock Exchange.

name: first-british-income-tax
description: Britain introduced the first income tax to fund the wars against Napoleonic France.

name: panic-of-1837
description: A US banking crisis triggered a deep depression, bank failures, and years of economic hardship.

name: rothschild-banking-empire
description: The Rothschild family financed governments across Europe, becoming the era's most powerful banking dynasty.

name: first-savings-bank
description: Scotland's Reverend Henry Duncan founded an early savings bank to help ordinary workers store money safely.

name: us-federal-income-tax
description: The Sixteenth Amendment let the US government levy a permanent federal income tax on its citizens.

name: gold-rush-currency-clipper
description: Nevada's Comstock Lode unearthed huge silver deposits that enriched the US and influenced its coinage.

name: first-travelers-cheque
description: The London Credit Exchange Company issued early traveler's cheques usable across European cities.

name: western-union-money-transfer
description: Western Union began wiring money by telegraph, letting people send funds across the country in minutes.

name: panic-of-1907
description: A New York banking panic, calmed by J.P. Morgan, exposed the need for a central bank in the US.

name: weimar-hyperinflation
description: German prices spiraled so fast that banknotes became nearly worthless and people burned cash for heat.

name: wall-street-crash-1929
description: The US stock market collapsed in October, wiping out fortunes and ushering in the Great Depression.

name: glass-steagall-act
description: The US separated commercial and investment banking and created deposit insurance after mass bank failures.

name: fdr-gold-confiscation
description: Roosevelt ordered Americans to surrender most gold coins and bullion as the US left the gold standard at home.

name: diners-club-charge-card
description: The Diners Club card let members charge restaurant meals, launching the modern payment-card industry.

name: first-credit-card-bankamericard
description: Bank of America mailed the BankAmericard, the first widely used revolving bank credit card, later becoming Visa.

name: first-eurodollar-market
description: US dollars held in European banks formed the eurodollar market, a vast pool of offshore international money.

name: decimal-currency-uk
description: On Decimal Day, Britain replaced its old pounds, shillings, and pence with a decimal pound of 100 pence.

name: smithsonian-agreement
description: Major nations realigned currencies after the US ended dollar-gold convertibility, ending fixed Bretton Woods rates.

name: nasdaq-launches
description: NASDAQ began as the world's first electronic stock market, trading shares through computers instead of a floor.

name: first-debit-card
description: US banks introduced early debit cards, letting customers pay directly from their checking accounts.

name: grameen-microcredit
description: Muhammad Yunus began lending tiny sums to poor villagers in Bangladesh, pioneering modern microfinance.

name: plaza-accord-signed
description: Five major economies agreed to weaken the US dollar against the yen and mark to fix trade imbalances.

name: japanese-asset-bubble-bursts
description: Japan's inflated stock and property prices collapsed, beginning a long economic stagnation.

name: black-wednesday-pound
description: Speculators forced the British pound out of Europe's exchange rate system, costing the government billions.

name: swiss-bank-secrecy-law
description: Switzerland made it a crime to reveal bank client details, cementing its reputation for secret banking.

name: dot-com-bubble-bursts
description: Inflated internet-company stock prices crashed, wiping out trillions in market value.

name: paypal-online-payments
description: PayPal launched to let people send money over the internet, helping power the rise of online commerce.

name: subprime-mortgage-crisis
description: Defaults on risky US home loans triggered a banking meltdown and the global financial crisis.

name: greek-debt-crisis
description: Greece needed massive bailouts as its government debt threatened the stability of the entire eurozone.

name: ethereum-smart-contracts
description: Ethereum launched a blockchain with smart contracts, enabling new digital currencies and finance apps.

name: contactless-payment-spreads
description: Tap-to-pay cards let shoppers buy goods by waving a card near a reader, speeding up everyday payments.

name: m-pesa-mobile-money
description: Kenya's M-Pesa let people send and store money on basic mobile phones, transforming finance in Africa.

name: gamestop-short-squeeze
description: Small online investors bid up GameStop shares, inflicting huge losses on hedge funds betting against it.

name: ftx-crypto-collapse
description: The major cryptocurrency exchange FTX failed amid fraud, wiping out billions in customer funds overnight.

name: roman-argentarii-banking
description: Roman argentarii ran private banks that took deposits, made loans, and changed money in the forum.
